title: Data Clustering in Microsoft Fabric Data Warehouse — A Practical Guide
page_content: Data Clustering in Microsoft Fabric Data Warehouse — A Practical GuideSubham Kalkhuriya9 min read·Just now--ListenShareStop scanning 200 million rows when you only need 2 millionThe ProblemYour Fabric Data Warehouse is set up. Reports are connected. But dashboards are slow. Queries are timing out.You check the query. It looks fine. The issue isn’t the SQL -it’s the storage. Your engine is scanning every single row in a 200 million row table just to return a few thousand records.This is exactly the problem Data Clustering solves.What is Data Clustering?Data Clustering is a way to physically organize your rows on disk so that similar data is stored close together.Without clustering, your rows are stored in the order they arrived — completely random from a query perspective.With clustering, rows with similar values in your chosen column are stored together in the same storage files. When a query filters on that column, the engine only opens the files it actually needs -and skips everything else.This skipping is called File Skipping, and it’s the core reason clustering makes queries faster.A Simple ExampleSay you have 5 files storing Sales data for 5 customers.Without clustering — data is scattered:File 1: Cust 1003, Cust 1001, Cust 1005, Cust 1002File 2: Cust 1004, Cust 1003, Cust 1001, Cust 1005File 3: Cust 1002, Cust 1004, Cust 1003, Cust 1001File 4: Cust 1005, Cust 1002, Cust 1004, Cust 1003File 5: Cust 1001, Cust 1005, Cust 1002, Cust 1004Query: WHERE CustomerID = 1003Cust 1003 is in all 5 files. Engine opens all 5. Full scan.With clustering on CustomerID — data is grouped:File 1: Cust 1001, Cust 1001, Cust 1001, Cust 1001File 2: Cust 1002, Cust 1002, Cust 1002, Cust 1002File 3: Cust 1003, Cust 1003, Cust 1003, Cust 1003  ← only this file neededFile 4: Cust 1004, Cust 1004, Cust 1004, Cust 1004File 5: Cust 1005, Cust 1005, Cust 1005, Cust 1005Same query: WHERE CustomerID = 1003Engine reads the metadata → knows Cust 1003 is only in File 3 → opens File 3 → skips 4 files = 80% less I/O.Why Use It? (Real Numbers)I tested this on the NY Taxi dataset — 76 million rows, same query, same data. Here’s what actually happened:Press enter or click to view image in full size64% less data processed. 36% less CPU. 50% faster. Just by adding CLUSTER BY.The regular table had a memory cache advantage — it had been queried before, so most of its data was already warm in RAM. The clustered table was freshly created, with some files still on remote storage. Despite that, the clustered version won by a significant margin. When you reduce total data scanned this aggressively, even caching advantages stop mattering.Press enter or click to view image in full sizeWhen Should You Use It?Use clustering when:Your table has millions of rows (larger the table, bigger the benefit)Your queries filter on specific columns frequently — dashboards, reports, scheduled jobsYour filter columns have many distinct values — like CustomerID, OrderDate, ProductIDSkip clustering when:Your table is small (overhead not worth it)The column you’d cluster on has very few distinct values — like Gender, Status, RegionYou only use that column in GROUP BY or JOIN, not in WHEREWhich Columns Can You Cluster On?Supported:Press enter or click to view image in full sizeNot supported:Press enter or click to view image in full sizeStep-by-Step: Practical ImplementationWe’ll use the NY Taxi dataset as our example throughout. Follow each step in order.Step 1-Set Up Your WarehouseWhat to do:Open your Microsoft Fabric workspace. If you don’t have a Warehouse yet, create one: New → Warehouse → give it a nameStep 2 -Load Sample DataWhat to do:We need a large table to see clustering’s real benefit. Load the NY Taxi dataset using this Article :https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/data-factory/tutorial-load-sample-data-to-data-warehouseOnce loaded, verify it’s there:SELECT COUNT(*) FROM nyctlcExpected output:Press enter or click to view image in full size76 million rows. Good. This is large enough to see a clear difference between clustered and non-clustered performance.Step 3 — Understand Your Query Before ClusteringWhat to do:Before clustering anything, know which query you’re trying to speed up. Here’s the query we’ll test:SELECT    YEAR(lpepPickupDatetime)  AS pickup_year,    AVG(fareAmount)           AS average_fareFROM nyctlcWHERE lpepPickupDatetime BETWEEN '2008-12-31' AND '2014-06-30'GROUP BY YEAR(lpepPickupDatetime)ORDER BY YEAR(lpepPickupDatetime) DESCOPTION (LABEL = 'Regular');Run this query once. Note how long it takes.The OPTION (LABEL = 'Regular') part tags this query so we can find it later in Query Insights for comparison.Why this column?The WHERE clause filters on lpepPickupDatetime. It's a datetime column with millions of distinct values (one per taxi trip). This makes it a perfect candidate for clustering -high cardinality, frequently filtered.Expected output:Press enter or click to view image in full sizeStep 4 — Create a Clustered Copy of the TableWhat to do:Now create a new version of the table with clustering on lpepPickupDatetime. We use CREATE TABLE AS SELECT (CTAS) to copy the data and apply clustering in one step:CREATE TABLE nyctlc_ClusteredWITH (CLUSTER BY (lpepPickupDatetime))AS SELECT * FROM nyctlc;This will take a few minutes — it’s copying 76 million rows and organizing them by datetime during ingestion.Expected output:Press enter or click to view image in full sizeStep 5 — Verify That Clustering is AppliedWhat to do:Before testing, confirm the clustering is correctly defined on your new table:SELECT    t.name                      AS table_name,    c.name                      AS column_name,    ic.data_clustering_ordinal  AS clustering_ordinalFROM sys.tables tJOIN sys.columns c    ON t.object_id = c.object_idJOIN sys.index_columns ic    ON c.object_id = ic.object_id   AND c.column_id = ic.column_idWHERE ic.data_clustering_ordinal > 0ORDER BY t.name, ic.data_clustering_ordinal;Expected output:Press enter or click to view image in full sizeYou should see your table name, the column name, and ordinal 1. If this row appears, clustering is active.Step 6 — Run the Same Query on the Clustered TableWhat to do:Run the exact same query, but this time on the clustered table. Tag it with 'Clustered' label:SELECT    YEAR(lpepPickupDatetime)  AS pickup_year,    AVG(fareAmount)           AS average_fareFROM nyctlc_ClusteredWHERE lpepPickupDatetime BETWEEN '2008-12-31' AND '2014-06-30'GROUP BY YEAR(lpepPickupDatetime)ORDER BY YEAR(lpepPickupDatetime) DESCOPTION (LABEL = 'Clustered');Run this query only once. You want to compare cold cache performance — if you run it multiple times, the cache kicks in and both versions will look equally fast, hiding clustering’s actual benefit.Expected output:Press enter or click to view image in full sizeSame 6 rows as before (one per year from 2009–2014), but it should complete noticeably faster.Step 7 — Measure the Difference with Query InsightsWhat to do:Wait 2–3 minutes for Query Insights to process the query history. Then run:SELECT    label,    total_elapsed_time_ms,    allocated_cpu_time_ms,    data_scanned_remote_storage_mb,    data_scanned_memory_mb,    data_scanned_disk_mb,    result_cache_hitFROM queryinsights.exec_requests_historyWHERE label IN ('Regular', 'Clustered')ORDER BY submit_time DESC;Actual results from my test run:Press enter or click to view image in full sizeWhat to look at:The most important column is not any single one -it’s remote_mb + memory_mb combined. That's the total data the engine actually touched to answer your query.Regular table touched 210 MB total — 208 MB from memory cache, 2 MB from remote storage.Clustered table touched 75.8 MB total — 70 MB from memory cache, 5.5 MB from remote storage.That’s 64% less data processed — and it directly shows up in the other numbers too: 36% less CPU, 50% faster end-to-end.Reading the ResultsAt first glance, the Clustered table shows more remote storage reads (5.4 MB) than the Regular table (2.0 MB) — which looks confusing. Let me explain why this actually makes perfect sense.The Regular table is older. It had been queried multiple times before this test, so most of its data was already sitting warm in memory — 208 MB of it. The engine barely touched remote storage because RAM had it covered.The Clustered table was freshly created. A small portion of its files hadn’t been cached yet, so the engine fetched those from remote storage — hence the 5.4 MB.But now look at the memory column. The Clustered table only needed 70 MB from memory, while Regular needed 208 MB.Add it up:Regular: 208 + 2 = 210 MB totalClustered: 70 + 5.4 = 75.8 MB totalThe Clustered table processed 64% less data overall — even though it had no cache warmup advantage. The engine simply knew which files to open and skipped the rest. That’s file skipping doing its job.The result: 50% faster end-to-end, 36% less CPU consumed — on the same 76 million row dataset, same query, same Fabric capacity.result_cache_hit — Both show 0. This confirms both queries actually ran and weren't served from a cached result. The timing is real.If your Query Insights query returns no rows, wait 5–10 minutes and try again. There’s a short processing delay before data appears.If your Query Insights query returns no rows, wait 5–10 minutes and try again. There’s a processing delay before data appears.Step 8 — Apply to Your Production TableNow apply what you learned to your own tables. Here’s the pattern:Scenario A — New table (you haven’t created it yet):CREATE TABLE FactSales (    SaleID      INT,    CustomerID  INT,    OrderDate   DATE,    Amount      DECIMAL(10,2)) WITH (CLUSTER BY (CustomerID, OrderDate));Use this when you’re designing a new table from scratch. Cluster on the columns that your WHERE clauses filter on most.Scenario B — Existing table (already has data, want to add clustering):-- Create a new clustered versionCREATE TABLE FactSales_ClusteredWITH (CLUSTER BY (CustomerID, OrderDate))AS SELECT * FROM FactSales;-- Once verified, rename: drop old, rename new-- (Do this during a maintenance window)You cannot add or change clustering on an existing table. CTAS is the only way to re-cluster.Scenario C — Check if a specific table has clustering:SELECT    c.name                      AS column_name,    ic.data_clustering_ordinal  AS ordinalFROM sys.tables tJOIN sys.columns c    ON t.object_id = c.object_idJOIN sys.index_columns ic    ON c.object_id = ic.object_id   AND c.column_id = ic.column_idWHERE t.name = 'FactSales_Clustered'  AND ic.data_clustering_ordinal > 0ORDER BY ic.data_clustering_ordinal;Limitations to Keep in MindDefined at creation only. You cannot add or modify clustering on an existing table. Use CTAS to recreate it.Max 4 columns. You can cluster on up to 4 columns. In practice, 1–2 is usually sufficient.IDENTITY columns not allowed. Use a different column in your CLUSTER BY.Ingestion is slightly slower. Clustering adds processing overhead on writes. The read savings more than compensate for most DW workloads.Small inserts may cluster asynchronously. If you insert fewer than 1 million rows at a time, clustering might happen in the background rather than immediately during ingestion.Column order in CLUSTER BY doesn’t matter. CLUSTER BY (A, B) and CLUSTER BY (B, A) produce the same result.Quick Reference — Dos and Don’tsPress enter or click to view image in full sizeWrapping UpData Clustering is one of the most impactful performance features in Fabric Data Warehouse — and it requires zero changes to your queries. Define it once at table creation, and every query that filters on that column benefits automatically.The numbers from this test tell the real story: the regular table had a warm memory cache advantage — most of its data was already in RAM before the query even ran. The clustered table was fresh, pulling some files from remote storage. Clustered still finished 50% faster, touching 64% less data total. When file skipping is working correctly, infrastructure advantages stop mattering.For production Gold layer tables that power dashboards and reports, clustering should be part of your default table design. Identify your high-cardinality filter columns, add CLUSTER BY at creation time, and let Query Insights confirm the impact.Have questions or want to share your clustering results? Drop a comment below.Follow for more hands-on Microsoft Fabric content.
